parsed.summary: The commit introduces an npm scope rename and a new interactive menu on the setup command. The scope rename is a breaking change for existing users: prior installations under the old scope will not update in-place and will silently diverge. The interactive menu on an already-configured setup path risks blocking non-interactive or scripted usage and has no visible guard for corrupted/partial existing config. Both issues are plausible under real usage and are not addressed in the diff.

findings.title: npm scope rename is a silent breaking change for existing installations
findings.body: The package.json and plugin.json changes rename the npm scope. Any user who installed the plugin under the previous scope will not receive this update via normal package manager flows; their install will silently stay on the old version. There is no visible migration path, deprecation notice, or postinstall redirect. The marketplace.json change compounds this by potentially registering under a new identifier while the old one remains published. Inference: based on 4-line changes to package.json and 2-line changes to plugin.json consistent with a scope rename, plus the commit message explicitly stating 'rename npm scope'.
findings.recommendation: Either keep the old scope and publish a deprecation redirect, or add a postinstall/preinstall script under the old package name that warns and points users to the new scope. Document the migration step prominently in the CHANGELOG and release_card. If the old package can be deprecated on the registry, do that before shipping.

findings.title: Interactive menu on re-setup may break non-interactive or scripted workflows
findings.body: The commands/setup.md change adds an interactive menu when setup is already configured (19 lines changed). If the command previously completed silently or idempotently on re-run, any CI/automation pipeline or scripted setup will now hang waiting for interactive input. There is no visible flag or environment-variable bypass for non-interactive mode. Inference: the commit message states 'add interactive menu to /glm:setup when already configured' and the 19-line diff to the markdown command file is consistent with adding prompt-based branching logic.
findings.recommendation: Add a non-interactive flag or environment variable (e.g., GLM_SETUP_NONINTERACTIVE=1 or --yes) that skips the menu and applies a default or specified action. Document this escape hatch in the command help text.

findings.title: Release card rewrite lacks verification of link consistency
findings.body: The release_card.md has 326 lines changed, representing a substantial rewrite. Any internal cross-references, URLs, or package name references that embed the old scope will now be stale or broken. A manual doc review can miss these, and there is no visible automated check. Inference: large documentation rewrite coinciding with a scope rename makes stale references likely.
findings.recommendation: Search the entire release_card.md for the old scope string and any hardcoded URLs referencing the old package name. Add a CI check that greps for the old scope across all markdown files to catch regressions.

next_steps: Verify the full diff with `git show 7766943` to confirm the old scope string is fully removed and no dangling references remain.
Confirm whether the old-scoped package is still published on the registry; if so, add a deprecation notice pointing to the new scope.
Add a non-interactive bypass to the setup menu and test it in a headless/scripted environment before shipping.
